narrative: Dear Alex, I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have your application turned away because our model predicts a 42.7% chance you'll struggle with the bar exam feels deeply unfair, especially when you've poured so much into getting here. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 decile of 2.0, which stood out as the strongest signal pulling toward potential failure, suggesting that even as you progressed, your class standing didn't climb as high as it typically does for those who pass on the first try. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 1.0, reinforcing a pattern of starting from the lower end of the pack academically, which the model saw as a persistent hurdle. Your middle family income bracket added another layer, as it often correlates with fewer resources for the intense prep that bar success demands. Even your solid LSAT score of 36.0 and undergraduate GPA of 3.1, while respectable, weren't enough to counterbalance those trends in the model's eyes—they nudged toward risk rather than reassurance. Factors like being a white male pursuing full-time studies played smaller roles, but together they painted a picture of someone whose academic trajectory and support structure mirror too many past students who faced bar setbacks. It's not that you're incapable—far from it—but this combination simply didn't align with the profiles of reliable bar passers in our data. The good news? You're targeting law school with clear intent, and with targeted improvements like boosting your deciles through stronger study habits or extra tutoring, you could shift this story entirely. Many in similar spots have turned it around—what's your next step? Hang in there; this is just one chapter.
